parentQuestion: A patient has an injury of the spine and is now suffering from a loss of motor function in his right arm. However, he still has normal sensory function in the arm. Based on this, the nervous tissue damage is likely located at ___.

scaffolds.q: Motor function means the ability to move muscles.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Motor = movement. Motor nerves control your muscles, allowing you to walk, grab things, and move your body. Loss of motor function means you can't move that body part.

scaffolds.q: Sensory function means the ability to feel sensations like touch, pain, and temperature.
scaffolds.explain: Exactly! Sensory nerves let you feel things - touch, pressure, pain, hot, cold. If sensory function is intact, you can still feel even if you can't move.
Dorsal root = sensory! Think 'D for Dorsal, D for Detecting.' The dorsal root brings sensory information INTO the spinal cord from the body.
Ventral root = motor! The ventral root carries motor commands OUT from the spinal cord to muscles. Remember: Ventral = 'Vroom' (movement)!

scaffolds.q: The arm is controlled by nerves from the ___ region of the spinal cord.
scaffolds.explain: The arm is controlled by cervical nerves (C5-T1). The neck region of your spinal cord controls your upper limbs. Makes sense - the arm nerves exit near shoulder level!

scaffolds.q: If you damage ONLY motor nerves, you would lose the ability to move but still be able to feel.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Motor and sensory pathways are separate. Damage only motor nerves = paralysis but you can still feel. Damage only sensory nerves = numbness but you can still move.

scaffolds.q: If someone can feel their arm but cannot move it, which pathway is damaged?
scaffolds.explain: The motor pathway! Since feeling is intact, sensory nerves work fine. Since movement is lost, motor nerves are damaged. The deficit tells you which pathway is broken.

scaffolds.q: Damage to the ventral root at cervical level would cause paralysis in the arm while preserving sensation.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Ventral root = motor only. Cervical level = arm control. So ventral root damage at cervical level = can't move arm but can still feel it. The dorsal (sensory) root is intact.

scaffolds.q: A patient with normal arm sensation but no arm movement most likely has damage to the ___ root at the ___ level.
scaffolds.explain: Ventral (motor) root at cervical (arm) level. Motor loss + sensory preservation = ventral root damage. Arm involvement = cervical level. Put them together: ventral root at cervical vertebrae.
